title: Commercial Roofing in Southern Shops, SC | Commercial Roofers of Spartanburg

html:
Light-Industrial Roofing Near Spartanburg's Old Rail Shops

The Southern Shops area takes its name from the Southern Railway repair shops that once operated here, and the light-industrial character that legacy left behind is still visible in the building stock: steel-frame structures with high clear spans and crane bays built for heavy equipment work, several of which have been repurposed for manufacturing, fabrication, or storage use over the years. Roofing on buildings like this differs from a standard retail or office roof in a few specific ways.

High Clear-Span Roof Structures

Buildings originally designed around crane bays and heavy shop equipment typically use steel roof trusses spanning wide open floor areas, with fewer interior columns to break up the roof deck. That structural profile generally handles roofing loads well, but it also means fewer natural drainage high points, so we pay close attention to slope and ponding patterns across a roof this size rather than assuming the original design accounted for modern drainage expectations.

Older built-up roofing on these structures has often been recoated or patched multiple times over the decades, and by the time we're called in, the honest assessment is usually full replacement rather than another round of patching a membrane that's exceeded its service life.

Vibration From Active Rail Lines

Active rail lines still run through and near this area, and buildings close to the tracks experience a low-level vibration that isn't present at a typical suburban commercial site. Over time, that vibration can work at membrane seams and mechanical fasteners differently than a static building would, particularly on older systems where seam adhesive has already aged. We check seam integrity more frequently on buildings in close proximity to active track than we would on a comparable roof elsewhere in the county.

Fully adhered or heat-welded seam systems generally hold up better under sustained low-level vibration than mechanically fastened systems, a factor we weigh into the system recommendation for buildings closest to the rail corridor.

Exhaust and Equipment Penetrations

Manufacturing and fabrication tenants in repurposed shop buildings often need rooftop penetrations for process exhaust, dust collection, or ventilation equipment that a standard office or retail building wouldn't require. Each of these penetrations needs to be flashed to the membrane manufacturer's specification and, on an older roof, checked against the deck's condition before cutting, since a penetration through a deteriorated deck section compounds an existing problem rather than just adding a new one.

  • Full roof condition assessment for aged built-up roofing systems
  • Ponding water and slope analysis on wide clear-span structures
  • Seam integrity inspection for buildings near active rail lines
  • Fully adhered or heat-welded membrane specification for vibration exposure
  • Process exhaust and dust-collection curb flashing to manufacturer detail
  • Deck condition verification before any new penetration cut

Modernizing Older Systems

A number of buildings in this area still carry roof systems installed decades ago, well past a reasonable service expectation for the materials involved. Modernizing these roofs usually means moving from built-up roofing to a single-ply TPO or EPDM system, which reduces long-term maintenance demands and gives the owner a clear, documented warranty rather than relying on an undocumented patch history.

We handle this transition as a full tear-off in most cases, since layering a new membrane over decades of built-up roofing rarely meets manufacturer warranty terms and doesn't address whatever deck issues have accumulated underneath.

Fall Protection on High Clear-Span Roofs

The taller roof structures common in this area, built for the crane bays and shop equipment of the original rail buildings, put crews working further from the ground than a typical single-story retail roof. We plan anchor points, guardrail placement, and access routes specific to each building's parapet height and roof geometry before work starts, documented as part of the project's safety plan rather than handled informally on site.

This planning also affects scheduling, since a roof this tall generally takes longer to stage material on and off of than a comparable square footage at a lower height, a factor we build into the timeline we give an owner up front. Crane or hoist access also needs to account for the rail right-of-way where a building sits close to active track, which limits where equipment can safely stage on some sites.

Questions From Southern Shops Facility Owners

Does the rail line vibration actually affect the roof?

Over years, yes, particularly at seams and mechanical fasteners. We recommend fully adhered or heat-welded systems for buildings closest to active track for that reason.

My roof has been patched for years. Is it time to replace it?

If it's an aged built-up system that's been repeatedly patched, that's usually a sign the underlying membrane has exceeded its service life and full replacement is the more cost-effective path.

Can you add exhaust equipment penetrations to an older roof?

We can, but we check the deck condition first. A penetration through a deteriorated deck section needs to be addressed as part of the same project, not treated separately.

What roofing system handles a wide clear-span building best?

Single-ply TPO or EPDM with a drainage layout matched to the actual roof slope, since these buildings often have fewer natural high points than a smaller structure.

Can you roof over my existing built-up system instead of a full tear-off?

Usually not, if the existing system has multiple layers or documented deck issues. A tear-off is typically required to qualify for a manufacturer warranty in that situation.
